questions.to_find: The ratio M1/M2 where M1 and M2 are cache misses experienced by P1 (row-major) and P2 (column-major) respectively.

understand.plain: We have a 32KB direct-mapped cache with 128-byte blocks. The array A is 512x512 of 8-byte doubles, totalling 2MB. P1 traverses A row by row (sequential memory order) while P2 traverses column by column (strided memory order). Because a cache block holds 16 elements (128/8), row-major access gets 16 hits after each miss, whereas column-major access strides 4096 bytes between consecutive accesses, causing almost every access to be a cache miss.

keywords.explain: Each memory block maps to exactly one cache line determined by (block address) mod (number of lines).
keywords.example: With 256 lines, block 0 and block 256 both map to line 0.

keywords.explain: When a block is loaded into cache on a miss, nearby elements in the same block can be accessed without further misses.
keywords.example: Accessing A[i][0] loads a 128-byte block, so A[i][1] through A[i][15] hit the cache.

keywords.explain: A memory access where the required data is not found in the cache, forcing a fetch from main memory.
keywords.example: First access to any cache block is always a compulsory miss.

understand.visual_alt: P1 (row-major) accesses memory sequentially so each cache block load covers 16 elements giving 1 miss per 16 accesses. P2 (column-major) jumps 4096 bytes between consecutive accesses (one full row), causing almost every access to miss.

given.aim: Compute the ratio of cache misses M1 (row-major) to M2 (column-major) for traversing a 512x512 array of 8-byte elements.

terms.term: Cache size = 32 KB, block size = 128 bytes
terms.meaning: Number of cache lines = 32768 / 128 = 256 lines (direct mapped).
terms.example: Line 0 holds bytes 0-127, line 1 holds bytes 128-255, etc.
terms.connects: Determines which memory blocks conflict in the cache.

terms.term: Array A: 512 x 512, 8 bytes per element
terms.meaning: Total size = 512 x 512 x 8 = 2 MB. Each row = 512 x 8 = 4096 bytes = 32 cache blocks.
terms.example: Row 0 occupies cache lines 0-31, row 1 occupies cache lines 32-63 (mod 256).
terms.connects: Row size vs cache size determines conflict behavior for column-major access.

terms.meaning: Sixteen consecutive 8-byte array elements fit in one 128-byte block.
terms.example: A[0][0] through A[0][15] share one block.
terms.connects: Row-major access gets 15 hits after every miss (spatial locality).

terms.meaning: Accessing A[0][j], A[1][j], ... jumps 512 x 8 = 4096 bytes = 32 cache lines between consecutive accesses.
terms.example: A[0][0] is in line (0 mod 256) = 0; A[1][0] is in line (32 mod 256) = 32.
terms.connects: After 8 rows, cache lines wrap around (8 x 32 = 256), so A[8][j] evicts the block loaded for A[0][j].

given.plan: For P1 (row-major), compute total blocks accessed = 512 x 512 / 16 = M1 misses. For P2 (column-major), because every column element resides in a different cache line and conflict misses occur after 8 rows, every access misses: M2 = 512 x 512. Divide M1 / M2.

steps.title: Compute M1 for P1 (row-major access)
steps.apply:
P1 iterates over A[i][j] in row order: A[0][0], A[0][1], ..., A[0][511], A[1][0], ... This is exactly the order data is laid out in memory (C row-major). Every 16 consecutive elements share one 128-byte block. The first element of each block causes a miss; the next 15 are hits.
M1 = (512 x 512) / 16 = 262144 / 16 = 16384 misses.
steps.note: This is ideal spatial locality: one compulsory miss loads the block and the next 15 accesses hit.

steps.title: Compute M2 for P2 (column-major access)
steps.formula_raw: Stride between A[i][j] and A[i+1][j] = 512 x 8 = 4096 bytes = 32 cache lines
steps.apply:
P2 iterates A[0][j], A[1][j], ..., A[511][j] for each j. Consecutive accesses in the inner loop are 4096 bytes apart (stride = 1 row). The cache has 256 lines. After 256/32 = 8 steps, line numbers repeat: A[0][j] and A[8][j] map to the same cache line, so A[8][j] evicts the block holding A[0][j]. When we revisit A[0][j]'s neighbors in the next column iteration the block has been evicted.
In effect, every access in the column traversal misses the cache.
M2 = 512 x 512 = 262144 misses.
steps.note: This is the worst case for direct-mapped caches: stride equal to a power-of-2 multiple of the cache size causes repeated conflict misses.

steps.title: Compute ratio M1/M2
steps.note: This equals 1/(elements per block) = 1/16, confirming that row-major is exactly 16 times better in cache performance.

questions.formula_note: Direct-mapped cache conflict analysis: row-major access exploits spatial locality (1 miss per cache block = 16 elements), while column-major access with stride = full row (4096 bytes) causes a cache conflict miss on virtually every access. Ratio = 1/(block_size / element_size) = 1/16.

questions.question:
Consider a 4-way set associative cache consisting of 128 lines with a line size of 64 words. The CPU generates a 20-bit address of a word in main memory. The number of bits in the TAG, LINE and WORD fields are respectively:

A. 9, 6, 5
B. 7, 7, 6
C. 7, 5, 8
D. 9, 5, 6
questions.to_find: Number of bits required for TAG, LINE (set index), and WORD fields in a 4-way set associative cache address.

understand.plain: In a set-associative cache, the CPU address is split into three parts: WORD (offset within a cache line), LINE (which set to look in), and TAG (to verify the correct block is cached). We use the given cache parameters to calculate each field's bit-width so they together add up to the full 20-bit address.

keywords.explain: The cache is divided into sets; each set holds k lines (k-way). A memory block maps to a specific set but can go into any of the k lines in that set.
keywords.example: 4-way, 32 sets: any block maps to one of 32 sets and can occupy any of 4 slots in that set.

keywords.explain: A physical address is divided into TAG | SET_INDEX | BLOCK_OFFSET. TAG identifies the memory block within a set; SET_INDEX selects the set; BLOCK_OFFSET selects the word within the line.
keywords.example: For 32 sets and 64-word lines with 20-bit address: 5 bits for set, 6 bits for offset, 9 bits for tag.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 500 160'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='500' height='160' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)' rx='8'/><text x='10' y='28' class='lbl ink'>20-bit address breakdown</text><rect x='10' y='40' width='180' height='50' class='no' rx='4'/><rect x='195' y='40' width='100' height='50' class='hi' rx='4'/><rect x='300' y='40' width='120' height='50' class='bg' rx='4'/><text x='85' y='60' class='tag' fill='white'>TAG</text><text x='225' y='60' class='tag ink'>LINE</text><text x='345' y='60' class='tag ink'>WORD</text><text x='85' y='78' class='sub' fill='white'>9 bits</text><text x='220' y='78' class='sub ink'>(set) 5 bits</text><text x='340' y='78' class='sub ink'>6 bits</text><text x='10' y='115' class='sub ink'>Total sets = 128 lines / 4 ways = 32 sets  |  LINE bits = log2(32) = 5</text><text x='10' y='135' class='sub ink'>Line size = 64 words  |  WORD bits = log2(64) = 6</text><text x='10' y='155' class='sub ink'>TAG bits = 20 - 5 - 6 = 9</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A 20-bit address split into three fields: TAG (9 bits, high), LINE/set-index (5 bits, middle), WORD/block-offset (6 bits, low). Derived from 32 sets and 64-word line size.

given.aim: Determine the number of bits allocated to TAG, LINE (set index), and WORD (block offset) fields in the cache address.

terms.meaning: Each set contains 4 lines. Number of sets = 128 / 4 = 32.
terms.example: With 4 ways and 32 sets: 4 x 32 = 128 total lines.
terms.connects: Number of sets determines the bits needed for the SET INDEX (LINE) field.

terms.meaning: Each cache line holds 64 words. WORD field must address any of the 64 positions within a line.
terms.example: 64 = 2^6, so 6 bits needed for the word offset.

terms.meaning: The total address space is 2^20 words. All address bits must be accounted for by TAG + LINE + WORD.
terms.example: If WORD=6 and LINE=5, then TAG = 20 - 6 - 5 = 9.
terms.connects: TAG bits are derived by subtracting known field widths from total address width.

given.plan: Calculate WORD bits from line size (log2(64)), calculate LINE bits from number of sets (log2(128/4) = log2(32)), then derive TAG bits = 20 - LINE - WORD.

steps.title: Calculate WORD (block offset) bits
steps.formula_raw: WORD bits = log2(line size in words) = log2(64)
steps.apply: Line size = 64 words. To address any one of 64 words within a line: WORD bits = log2(64) = 6 bits.
steps.note: The WORD field selects which word within the fetched cache line we want.

steps.title: Calculate LINE (set index) bits
steps.formula_raw: Number of sets = total lines / associativity = 128 / 4 = 32; LINE bits = log2(32)
steps.apply: Total lines = 128, associativity = 4 (4-way). Number of sets = 128 / 4 = 32. Bits for set index = log2(32) = 5 bits.
steps.note: The LINE field (set index) determines which set of 4 lines to search in.

steps.title: Calculate TAG bits
steps.formula_raw: TAG bits = total address bits - LINE bits - WORD bits
steps.note: The TAG is stored in the cache to verify that the correct memory block is currently held in the set.

steps.title: State the answer
steps.apply: Summarising: WORD = 6 bits, LINE (set index) = 5 bits, TAG = 9 bits. The question asks for TAG, LINE, WORD in that order: 9, 5, 6. This corresponds to option D.
steps.note: Always compute fields in order: WORD from line size, LINE from number of sets, TAG as remainder. The 4-way associativity reduces the number of sets (and hence LINE bits) but does not affect TAG or WORD directly.

solution.result: TAG = 9 bits, LINE (set index) = 5 bits, WORD (block offset) = 6 bits. Answer: D. 9, 5, 6

questions.formula_note: Set-associative cache address decomposition: WORD bits = log2(line size), SET bits = log2(total lines / associativity), TAG bits = total address bits - SET bits - WORD bits.
